questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of ABSTAIN:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: India's energy demand is projected to grow at 3-4% annually over the next decade. While coal remains the dominant source, accounting for about 55% of installed capacity, renewable energy is expanding rapidly. Solar power capacity has grown from just 2.6 GW in 2014 to over 70 GW in 2024. Wind power contributes another 45 GW. The government's Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for solar manufacturing aims to reduce dependence on imports. Nuclear power, with 7.5 GW capacity, and hydropower, with 47 GW, are other important components of India's energy portfolio.

Coal accounts for about what percentage of India's installed capacity?

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of VENERATE:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Gondwana coal fields of eastern India contain the country's best quality coal. These fields stretch across Jharkhand, West Bengal, Odisha, and Chhattisgarh. The coal found here is primarily bituminous, which has high calorific value and low ash content. In contrast, the Tertiary coal fields of northeastern India contain lignite and sub-bituminous coal. Coal grading is based on ash content, with higher grades having lower ash percentages. Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

The coal found in Gondwana fields is primarily:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of ALOOF:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late 18th century, was powered largely by coal. Steam engines, fuelled by coal, drove factories, railways, and ships, transforming economies and societies. The demand for coal led to the expansion of mining operations across Europe and America. However, the social cost was high, with poor working conditions, child labour, and environmental pollution. The legacy of the Industrial Revolution continues to influence modern industrial practices and environmental policies. Today, many former mining regions are being revitalised through diversification and clean energy initiatives.

The word 'legacy' in the passage most nearly means:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of RESILIENT:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem:
Passage: Coal is the most abundant fossil fuel in India and plays a crucial role in the country's energy mix. India has the fourth-largest coal reserves in the world, with an estimated 344 billion tonnes. Coal India Limited (CIL) is the largest coal-producing company globally, accounting for about 82% of India's coal output. However, concerns about environmental degradation and climate change have prompted a shift towards cleaner energy sources like solar and wind power. The government has set a target of achieving 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030.

The word 'abundant' in the passage most nearly means:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of EXUBERANT:

The government's Production-Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for solar manufacturing aims to reduce dependence on imports. Nuclear power, with 7.5 GW capacity, and hydropower, with 47 GW, are other important components of India's energy portfolio.

India's installed nuclear power capacity is:

Washeries are used to reduce ash content and improve coal quality before it is supplied to power plants and industries.

India has the fourth-largest coal reserves in the world, with an estimated 344 billion tonnes. Coal India Limited (CIL) is the largest coal-producing company globally, accounting for about 82% of India's coal output. However, concerns about environmental degradation and climate change have prompted a shift towards cleaner energy sources like solar and wind power. questions.stem:
Passage: Coal is the most abundant fossil fuel in India and plays a crucial role in the country's energy mix. India has the fourth-largest coal reserves in the world, with an estimated 344 billion tonnes. Coal India Limited (CIL) is the largest coal-producing company globally, accounting for about 82% of India's coal output. However, concerns about environmental degradation and climate change have prompted a shift towards cleaner energy sources like solar and wind power. The government has set a target of achieving 500 GW of renewable energy capacity by 2030.

According to the passage, India has the ____ largest coal reserves in the world.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: The price of these goods have increased.
options: The price of these goods has increased.
The price of these goods are increased.
The prices of these goods has increased.
The price of these goods have increased.
questions.solution: The price of these goods has increased.

options: I am looking forward for meeting you.
I look forward to meet you.
I am looking forward to meeting you.
I am looking forward to meet you.
questions.solution: I am looking forward to meeting you.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Semantic analysis checks:
options: Type compatibility, scope rules, and other context-sensitive checks
questions.solution: Semantic analysis: types, declarations, scope, etc. (annotated AST).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(4) (with fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1) makes how many calls to the base cases fib(0) or fib(1)?
questions.solution: The number of base-case calls for fib(n) equals fib(n+1) = 5, illustrating exponential blow-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Durability ensures:
options: Committed transaction changes survive system failures
questions.solution: Durability: write-ahead logging (WAL) ensures committed changes can be recovered after crash.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NAND gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 1, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: LR parsing is:
questions.solution: LR(k) = Left-to-right scan, Rightmost derivation, k lookahead. Bottom-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 5 elements is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: INNER JOIN returns:
options: Only rows with matching values in both tables
questions.solution: INNER JOIN = intersection. LEFT JOIN = all left + matches from right.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A flip-flop stores:
questions.solution: FF: stores 1 bit (Q and Q' outputs). SR, JK, D, T types.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Register allocation by graph coloring:
options: Colors the interference graph with k colors (k = available registers)
questions.solution: Chaitin-Briggs algorithm: build interference graph → coalesce → simplify → spill → color.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of comparisons in selection sort for n=32 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An INNER JOIN returns:
options: Rows with matching values in both tables
questions.solution: INNER JOIN: intersection of both tables on join condition.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the XOR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An abstract syntax tree (AST) is:
options: A simplified parse tree (operators as internal nodes, operands as leaves)
questions.solution: AST: condensed form of parse tree (no intermediate nodes for single productions).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bubble sort has a worst-case time complexity of:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes ~n²/2 comparisons in the worst case.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A correlated subquery:
options: References columns from the outer query (executed for each outer row)
questions.solution: Correlated subquery: re-executed for each row of outer query (less efficient).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the AND gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 0, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=0</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">AND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Strength reduction replaces:
options: Expensive operations with cheaper ones (e.g., multiply → shift)
questions.solution: Strength reduction: i*2 → i<<1, i^2 → i*i (or table lookup).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Kruskal's algorithm uses which data structure?
questions.solution: Kruskal = sort edges by weight + union-find for cycle detection.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The HAVING clause filters:
options: Groups after GROUP BY (like WHERE for groups)
questions.solution: HAVING = filter groups (WHERE filters individual rows before grouping).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 4-bit ripple counter counts from:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Constant folding is:
options: Evaluating constant expressions at compile time
questions.solution: Constant folding: x = 2 + 3 → x = 5 (computed at compile time).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of nodes in a complete binary tree of height h=11 (root at 0) is at most:
questions.solution: Max nodes in complete binary tree of height h = 2^(h+1)-1 = 4095.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SQL WHERE clause filters:
questions.solution: WHERE = row filter (pre-grouping). HAVING = group filter (post-grouping).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which logic gate outputs 1 only when all inputs are 1?
questions.solution: AND gives 1 only when every input is 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A symbol table stores:
options: Information about identifiers (names, types, scopes, memory locations)
questions.solution: Symbol table = identifier attributes (name, type, scope, offset, etc.).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bipartite graph check uses:
options: BFS/DFS with 2-coloring (alternating colors; conflict → not bipartite)
questions.solution: A graph is bipartite iff it has no odd-length cycles. 2-coloring via BFS.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A composite index is:
options: An index on a single column
questions.solution: Composite/compound index: multiple keys (column order matters for prefix queries).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A latch is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Control flow graph (CFG) nodes are:
questions.solution: CFG = directed graph where nodes = basic blocks, edges = possible control flow paths.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Ford-Fulkerson algorithm computes:
questions.solution: Ford-Fulkerson: max flow using augmenting paths.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: NoSQL databases are characterized by:
options: Flexible schema, horizontal scaling, and eventual consistency

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A magnitude comparator compares:
questions.solution: Magnitude comparator: A > B, A = B, A < B outputs. 7485 = 4-bit comparator.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An LR(0) item is:
options: A production with a dot indicating the parsing position
questions.solution: LR item: A → α·β (dot = current parsing position within the production).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Prim's algorithm is similar to:
questions.solution: Both Prim and Dijkstra use a similar greedy approach with priority queues.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The isolation level READ UNCOMMITTED allows:
options: Dirty reads (reading uncommitted changes from other transactions)
questions.solution: READ UNCOMMITTED: lowest isolation. Dirty read, non-repeatable read, phantom read all possible.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NAND gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Trace scheduling:
options: A global scheduling technique that follows frequent execution paths (traces)
questions.solution: Trace scheduling: pick most likely path (trace), schedule across basic blocks along trace.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of comparisons in selection sort for n=64 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: EXISTS vs IN: EXISTS returns:
options: True if the subquery returns any rows (better for large subqueries)
questions.solution: EXISTS: short-circuits on first match. IN: collects all results then compares.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the OR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inherited attributes in attribute grammars:
options: Pass values from parent to child in the parse tree

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Every recursive function must have a:
questions.solution: Without a base (terminating) case, recursion never stops and overflows the stack.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control):
options: Maintains multiple versions of each data item for concurrent access without blocking reads
questions.solution: MVCC: readers see snapshot (no locks), writers create new version. Used by PostgreSQL, Oracle, InnoDB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The output of a NOR gate is 1 when:
questions.solution: NOR: output = (A+B)'. Only 1 when A=0 and B=0.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bottom-up parsing uses:
questions.solution: Bottom-up (LR family): builds parse tree from leaves to root (reverse rightmost).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Cook-Levin theorem proved:
questions.solution: Cook-Levin (1971): SAT is NP-complete (first NP-complete problem).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A primary key in a relation must be:
questions.solution: A primary key uniquely identifies each tuple and cannot be null.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Boolean expression A + AB simplifies to:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A shift action in LR parsing:
options: Pushes the next input symbol and a state onto the stack
Replaces the top of the stack
questions.solution: Shift: read next token, push token + goto state. Reduce: pop RHS, push LHS + goto.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 10 elements is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normalization 1NF requires:
options: Atomic values (no repeating groups, each cell has single value)
questions.solution: 1NF: eliminate repeating groups (each attribute contains atomic values).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the AND gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">AND</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Copy propagation replaces:
options: Copies (x = y) with direct use of y (reduces register pressure)
questions.solution: Copy propagation: after x = y, replace uses of x with y (may enable dead code elimination of x).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The stable marriage problem uses:
questions.solution: Gale-Shapley: O(n²) deferred acceptance algorithm (guarantees stable matching).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Log-based recovery (UNDO/REDO):
options: Records changes in a log; UNDO to revert uncommitted, REDO to replay committed after crash
questions.solution: Write-Ahead Logging (WAL): log must be written before data. ARIES: steal + no-force + WAL + REDO + UNDO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 4:1 multiplexer has how many select lines?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Parser generators like YACC use:
questions.solution: YACC/Bison: LALR(1) parser. Lex: DFA-based lexer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: OSPF uses:
questions.solution: OSPF = link-state (SPF tree), fast convergence, supports VLSM.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A linear bounded automaton (LBA) recognizes:
questions.solution: LBA: TM with tape bounded by input length (k×n). CSL ↔ NSPACE(n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'starvation' condition occurs when:
options: A process is indefinitely delayed despite being runnable
questions.solution: Starvation = indefinite postponement (e.g., low priority process never gets CPU).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: 1's complement of binary 1010 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum transmission unit (MTU) of Ethernet is:
questions.solution: Ethernet MTU = 1500 bytes (max payload of Ethernet frame).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which data structure is used for implementing LRU cache?
questions.solution: LRU cache: DLL for order + hash map for O(1) lookup.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dependent types:
options: Types that depend on values (e.g., vector of length n)
Types that depend on other types
questions.solution: Dependent types: Vec A n (type depends on value n). Used in Idris, Agda, Coq. Allows proving properties.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Red-black tree properties ensure a height of:
questions.solution: Red-black tree ensures log₂(n+1) ≤ height ≤ 2 log₂(n+1).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Load balancing in multiprocessor systems:
questions.solution: Load balancing: push migration (load checker moves tasks) or pull migration (idle CPU pulls).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of comparisons in selection sort for n=100 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A link of 100 Mbps has a round-trip time of 50 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Data hazards are resolved by:
options: Forwarding/bypassing (results from EX/MEM/WB to EX input)
questions.solution: Forwarding: ALU result from EX stage forwarded to next instruction's EX input.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The time complexity of binary search for n=100 is:
questions.solution: Binary search halves the search space each step: O(log n) ≈ O(log 100).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Using Master Theorem, T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) solves to:
questions.solution: a=2, b=2, f(n)=O(n). Case 2: T(n)=O(n log n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of mergesort for n=256 is:
questions.solution: Mergesort always O(n log n). For n=256: O(256 log 256).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Quick sort worst-case occurs when:
options: The pivot is always the minimum or maximum element
questions.solution: Worst case: pivot always min or max = O(n²). Sorted array with first element as pivot = O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: To find shortest paths when some edge weights are negative (but no negative cycle), use:
questions.solution: Bellman–Ford handles negative edges and detects negative cycles; Dijkstra cannot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Binary search on a sorted array of 1024 elements needs at most how many comparisons?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The time complexity of binary search for n=16 is:
questions.solution: Binary search halves the search space each step: O(log n) ≈ O(log 16).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 5 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The subnet mask 255.255.255.240 has:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Binary search on a sorted array of 64 elements needs at most how many comparisons?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A complete binary tree with n nodes has a height of:
questions.solution: Height = ⌊ log₂(n+1) ⌋ - 1 for a complete binary tree.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A disk block cache uses:
options: Recently used blocks are cached in memory to reduce disk I/O
questions.solution: Buffer cache: kernel caches disk blocks in main memory for faster access.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The BIT (Fenwick tree) supports:
options: Prefix sum queries and point updates in O(log n)
questions.solution: Fenwick tree: prefix sum O(log n), point update O(log n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 100 MB at 250 MB/s takes about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The offsetof macro:
options: Returns the byte offset of a member within a struct type
Returns the alignment of a member
Returns the address of a member
Returns the size of a member
questions.solution: offsetof(type, member): from <stddef.h>. Useful for manual serialization and container_of.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Microprogramming is:
options: Implementing the control unit using micro-instructions stored in control memory
questions.solution: Microprogrammed control: ROM stores microcode for each instruction (easier to design).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the hexadecimal number 40₁₆ is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The producer-consumer problem can be solved using:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of mergesort for n=8 is:
questions.solution: Mergesort always O(n log n). For n=8: O(8 log 8).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A red-black tree ensures:
options: All leaves are at same depth
No path is more than twice as long as any other (balanced)
questions.solution: Red-black tree: root is black, no adjacent red nodes, all paths have same # black nodes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A stack overflow occurs when:
options: Recursion depth exceeds the available stack memory
questions.solution: Excessive recursion causes stack overflow (each call pushes to stack).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The best-case time complexity of quicksort is:
questions.solution: Best case (balanced partition) = O(n log n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Test-driven development (TDD) follows:
options: Red (write failing test) → Green (write code to pass) → Refactor
questions.solution: TDD: write test before code (short development cycle: test-code-refactor).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Refactoring is:
options: Restructuring existing code without changing its external behavior
questions.solution: Refactoring: improve design, readability, maintainability. Should be backed by tests.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The LALR(1) parsing table is constructed from:
options: LR(1) items (merging states with same core)
questions.solution: LALR = Look-Ahead LR. Merges LR(1) states with identical LR(0) cores. May introduce reduce-reduce conflicts.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An orphan process is:
options: A child whose parent has terminated
questions.solution: Orphan = parent died first. Adopted by init (PID 1).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The pumping lemma for CFL is used to prove:
questions.solution: Pumping lemma: if L is CFL, strings ≥ p can be pumped. Contrapositive: can't pump → not CFL.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: SMTP is used for:
questions.solution: SMTP = send mail. POP3/IMAP = receive mail.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Ambiguous grammar:
options: Produces more than one parse tree for some string
questions.solution: Ambiguity: different leftmost/rightmost derivations → different parse trees. E.g., dangling else.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: T(n) = 2T(n/4) + √n solves to:
questions.solution: Master theorem: a=2, b=4, f(n)=n^(1/2). Compare: n^(log₄2) = √n. Case 2: O(√n log n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A queue follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: Elements leave a queue in the same order they entered — FIFO.
